original_abstract:
Iron (Fe) is a fundamental element for life, yet it exists in a delicate physiological balance where deficiency and excess both pose serious health risks. A similar homeostatic necessity applies to other essential minerals like calcium (Ca) and magnesium (Mg), as well as trace elements such as copper (Cu), zinc (Zn), lead (Pb), cadmium (Cd), mercury (Hg), and nickel (Ni). Many of these elements share common molecular pathways for absorption and transport. For instance, Cu and Cd can actively inhibit the uptake of Fe, while an excess of Fe can impair Cu metabolism and reduce ceruloplasmin (Cp) levels. Furthermore, high concentrations of Fe can hinder Zn absorption, and transferrin (Trf) serves as a shared transport protein for both Zn and Ni. 

Regulatory interactions also occur via specific ion channels; Ca inhibits the divalent metal transporter 1 (DMT1) in a dose-dependent manner—reducing Fe absorption—while low Mg levels significantly exacerbate the severity of Fe deficiency. Among toxic metals, Pb competitively interferes with Fe distribution, and elevated Cd absorption directly impairs Fe uptake. Mercury exposure is frequently associated with abnormally high ferritin levels, and Ni has been shown to alter intracellular Fe metabolism. Clinically, the removal of excess iron via phlebotomy in hemochromatosis patients has been found to inadvertently increase levels of Cd and Pb, while various anemias can alter several trace element profiles. Despite these known connections, the long-term health consequences of chronic exposure to most of these trace elements remain poorly understood and require further investigation.
humanized_abstract:
Iron (Fe) is important for life, but its homeostasis is very tight and is in imbalance with dramatic health consequences either with lack or excess of iron, and similarly with other essential minerals, including calcium and magnesium and trace elements like copper (Cu), zinc (Zn), lead (Pb), cadmium (Cd), mercury (Hg), and nickel (Ni). Many of the trace elements mentioned here share important overlapping mechanisms and pathways in intestinal absorption and plasma transport. For example, copper (Cu) inhibits iron (Fe) intestinal absorption and also as does cadmium (Cd) its absorptions, and excess iron (Fe) inhibits copper (Cu) metabolism with reductions in ceruloplasmin (Cp). Also, iron (Fe) excess inhibits zinc (Zn) intestinal absorption. Moreover, transferrin (Trf) is a protein for both zinc (Zn) and nickel (Ni) transport in the plasma.

Regulatory interactions with other elements can also occur through specific ion channels. For example, calcium (Ca) inhibits expression of divalent metal transporter 1 (DMT1) in a concentration-dependent manner and negatively affects iron absorption. Iron deficiency is markedly increased with low magnesium (Mg) levels. Lead (Pb) can disrupt iron distribution, and high levels of cadmium (Cd) absorption can hinder iron uptake. Mercury (Hg) is often associated with elevated ferritin. Nickel (Ni) disrupts iron handling within cells. Examples of clinical effects are also noted. Phlebotomy undertaken to reduce excess iron in individuals with hemochromatosis has been shown to increase levels of Cd and Pb. Certain types of anemia are also associated with changes in trace element levels. However, very little is known regarding long-term health effects of chronic exposure to most trace elements.
